title: Document

child_sections.title: Interim Report – Document Intelligence Refinery
child_sections.summary: The "Document Intelligence Refinery" interim report focuses on addressing the challenges of ingesting **heterogeneous financial documents** – including **PDFs, legal docs, & fiscal summaries** – for use in **Modern Financial Data Engineering (FDE)**. The goal is to transform these diverse sources into **auditable and queryable data**. This report details initial work ("Phase 0 Deliverable") outlining the **problem context** for this refinery process.

child_sections.title:  Line statistics: presence of pipe (|) characters, short-line frequency.
child_sections.summary: This section details line-level statistics used for document classification, specifically looking at the occurrence of pipe characters (|) and the frequency of short lines. These statistics are combined with keyword signals (financial, legal, technical, medical, general) to determine a document's "origin type." The initial step in this process classifies documents as “scanned_image” based on low character density and high image ratio thresholds.

child_sections.title:  medical: “patient”, “diagnosis”, “clinical”, …
child_sections.summary: This section outlines the process for information extraction, specifically within a "medical" domain focusing on entities like “patient”, “diagnosis”, and “clinical” data. The system prioritizes the highest-scoring domain, defaulting to "general" if none are strongly identified. Extraction cost is estimated based on input type – requiring a vision model for scanned images and tiered mapping (needs_vision_model) depending on document layout complexity (table-heavy, multi-column, or mixed).

child_sections.title:  Symptoms: Pipe- or grid-like tables become linear text, columns merge, numeric
child_sections.summary: This section describes a symptom where structured tables (pipe or grid-like) are incorrectly rendered as unstructured linear text, leading to column merging and misaligned numeric data. This impacts the reliability of querying numeric facts and hinders Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) by causing it to miss table context. The proposed mitigation involves detecting pipe-delimited lines during text extraction and explicitly identifying suspected tables for better processing.

child_sections.title:  Every LDU carries:
child_sections.summary: Each Logical Document Unit (LDU) contains metadata including `page_refs` – linking to the source document name, page number, and bounding box – as well as a `content_hash` for stable identification. A `ProvenanceChain`, detailing the origin of each `QueryAnswer`, is also attached to every answer and consists of a list of `ProvenanceItem` with similar metadata (document name, page number, bbox, content hash). This system tracks document origins and content integrity within the LDU structure.

child_sections.title: 1.4 Pipeline Diagram (Mermaid)
child_sections.summary:
Please provide the content of section "1.4 Pipeline Diagram (Mermaid)". I need the actual Mermaid diagram code or a textual description of the pipeline it represents to summarize it for you. 

Without the diagram's details, I can only state that this section *describes a data processing pipeline visually using the Mermaid syntax*. It likely outlines the stages involved in transforming data from source(s) to destination(s), and identifies key entities/components within that process.

child_sections.title: 2. Architecture Diagram & 5-Stage Pipeline
child_sections.summary: The architecture utilizes a 5-stage pipeline to process PDF documents, beginning with the **Triage Agent** which classifies input **Raw PDFs**. This initial stage outputs a **DocumentProfile** (saved as JSON) containing metadata like `origin_type`, `layout_complexity`, `domain_hint`, and `estimated_extraction_cost` – key data points for subsequent pipeline stages. The document profile essentially defines the characteristics of the document to guide further processing.

child_sections.title: This profile is the contract that governs routing in Stage 2.
child_sections.summary: This profile defines the routing rules for Stage 2 of a document processing pipeline. Stage 2 utilizes a multi-strategy extraction layer, taking a `document_path` as input and producing an `ExtractedDocument` along with a ledger entry as output. It employs three strategies – including `FastTextExtractor` using `pypdf` – to extract text and bounding box information from documents.

child_sections.title:  Later: plug in LM Studio VLM/OCR here (render PDF pages to images, send to mod
child_sections.summary: This section details integrating LM Studio's VLM/OCR capabilities to extract text from PDF pages by rendering them as images and sending them to a language model. The process results in an `ExtractedDocument` containing the parsed text. A router dynamically selects between extraction methods (A, B, C) based on estimated cost, applying confidence checks and budget limits per document.

child_sections.title:  content: chunk text (paragraph, list, table flattening, or caption).
child_sections.summary: This document section describes a “content” chunk – a discrete piece of text extracted from a larger document, which can be a paragraph, list, table, or caption. Each chunk is categorized by its `chunk_type` (text, table, figure, or list) and linked to its source location via `page_refs` including document name, page number, and bounding box coordinates. Key metadata includes the `bounding_box` of the chunk itself, a reference to its `parent_section`, and an approximate `token_count`.

child_sections.title:  For each section:
child_sections.summary: Each document section is processed by determining its page range based on Logical Document Units (LDUs) and extracting key entities – specifically capitalized words likely representing names or terms. A concise summary is then created using the first N words of the section's content, and these sections are organized as children under a top-level "Document" node. Finally, a vector store utilizing TF/cosine similarity is employed for data storage and retrieval.

child_sections.title:  Restrict semantic search to pages in these sections.
child_sections.summary: This section details restricting semantic search results to specific pre-defined **sections** of documents. The process involves collecting top text **chunks** from those sections and synthesizing an answer using both extractive and concatenative methods. A **ProvenanceChain** is then built for each hit, tracking the **document name**, **page number**, **bounding box (bbox)**, and **content hash** to ensure auditability.

child_sections.title: Token pricing assumptions (illustrative):
child_sections.summary: This section outlines illustrative token pricing for two AI models: a text model at $0.25 per 1 million tokens and a vision model (for PDFs) costing an equivalent of $1.00 per 1 million tokens due to larger image file sizes. A budget guard ("b") is also established, though its specifics aren't detailed here. These costs are intended to be relatively low, potentially utilizing local or hosted models.

child_sections.title: Cost estimate per document (P pages):
child_sections.summary: The estimated cost per document with P pages is based on approximately 1,000-1,500 tokens per page, leading to roughly 30-45k tokens for a 30-page document. At a rate of $0.25 per million tokens, this translates to an approximate cost of \$0.01125 per document; however, a typical range considering retries and longer prompts is \$0.01 – \$0.05 for documents between 30-100 pages.

child_sections.title:  Tokens: 75–100k.
child_sections.summary: This section details the cost of processing documents using tokens, with **75–100k tokens** required per document. The associated cost is **$0.075–0.10 USD**, but can range from $0.03 to $0.15 depending on page count (20-50 pages) and retries. A budget guard of **$0.20** is implemented to prevent exceeding costs, even for the most expensive 50-page scanned documents.

child_sections.title:  Route: Primarily B for table-quality extraction.
child_sections.summary: This route ("B") is focused on extracting data for table-quality results. It's categorized as "Strategy B" requiring careful table processing, with an estimated cost of $0.02–$0.06 per document. Costs are expected to stay below $0.10, and always within a $0.20 budget limit, even with potential escalation for complex layouts.
